title: Time Series Folds (Rolling Windows)
concept: Time series cross-validation uses rolling or expanding windows to train only on past data and evaluate on future data, avoiding temporal leakage.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What problem does Stratified K-Fold solve when evaluating a classifier?

options.text: It prevents folds from randomly missing rare target classes by preserving class ratios.
It sorts the data by time to make predictions more chronological.
It removes outliers from the training set automatically.

exercises.explain: Correct! Stratification keeps class ratios consistent. But what if our data is ordered by time, and future data points depend on past ones?

cards.title: Cheating with the future
cards.body: Imagine predicting tomorrow's weather by looking at the weather report from next week. That would be cheating! In standard cross-validation, data is shuffled randomly, meaning your model might train on future data to predict the past.

cards.title: What is Time Series Split?
cards.body: Time Series Split (or rolling/expanding window validation) is a method where we respect the temporal order of data. We train only on past data and validate on future data.

cards.title: Reading a mystery novel
cards.body: When reading a mystery novel, you can't read chapter 5 to help you guess what happens in chapter 3. You must read chronologically. In time series validation, the model's history must grow forward in time.

cards.title: Expanding windows
cards.body: In an expanding window split, Fold 1 trains on Month 1 to validate on Month 2. Fold 2 trains on Months 1-2 to validate on Month 3. Fold 3 trains on Months 1-3 to validate on Month 4. The training window expands forward.

cards.title: Is random shuffling good?
cards.body: We are taught that random shuffling is great for training models. But for time series, random shuffling is disastrous. It leaks future information into the past, making the model look artificially accurate.

cards.title: Random vs Time Series Splits
cards.body: How training and validation sets differ for time-based data:

left.text:
Shuffled data.
Future leaks into past.
Unrealistic time predictions.

right.text:
Chronological order.
Train on past, test on future.
Honest time predictions.

exercises.prompt: Why is random shuffling bad for time series cross-validation?

options.text: It causes future data to leak into the training set, leading to unrealistic validation scores.
It increases the number of features in the dataset.
It deletes old data points to make room for new ones.
It forces the model to use L1 regularization instead of L2.

exercises.explain: Shuffling mixes past and future. If a model trains on future data, it gets an unrealistic advantage that won't exist in production.

exercises.prompt: True or false: In time series cross-validation, the training set must always occur chronologically before the validation set.
exercises.explain: True! To simulate real-world usage, we must always train on past data and validate on future data.

exercises.sentence: Time series cross-validation uses an ___ window to add more past data to the training set over time.
exercises.explain: An expanding window grows chronologically, incorporating more historical data as we move forward in time.

exercises.prompt: Which of these setups represents a valid time series cross-validation fold?

options.text: Training on January through June data, and validating on July data.
Training on January, March, and July data, and validating on February data.
Training on December data, and validating on October data.

exercises.explain: Training on past data (Jan-Jun) and validating on future data (Jul) strictly respects the arrow of time.

exercises.prompt: You are building a model to predict daily retail sales. Using a standard random K-Fold split, your validation accuracy is 98%. However, when you deploy the model, it gets only 55% accuracy on new sales data. What went wrong?

options.text: Random K-Fold leaked future sales patterns into the past training data, hiding the model's inability to forecast actual future trends.
The learning rate was too high for production data.
You should have deleted all historical sales data before deploying.
The model overfitted to the class labels of the test set.

exercises.explain: Random splits leak future trends into past training. When deployed, the future is unknown, and the model fails. Time series splits would have caught this! 🕰️
